result:
Subject: Yesterday's payments outage — what happened and what we're doing about it

Yesterday, our payments service was unavailable for approximately four hours. During that window, some of you saw transactions fail, get stuck mid-flow, or return errors at checkout. That is not the experience you trust us to deliver, and we're sorry. If your business or your customers were affected, the responsibility for that sits with us.

The root cause was a misconfigured cache invalidation rule that, under a traffic spike, caused our payment authorization layer to repeatedly evict and rebuild critical state. As load climbed, the service degraded until transactions began failing outright. Once we identified the faulty rule, we rolled it back, restored cache stability, and confirmed full recovery before standing down our incident response.

To prevent a repeat, we're making two concrete changes. First, configuration changes to the payments path will now go through canary rollouts, so a bad rule reaches a small slice of traffic — not all of it — before we catch it. Second, we're expanding chaos testing in staging to specifically exercise cache invalidation and traffic-spike scenarios, so this class of failure is caught before it ever reaches production.

If you were impacted, we'd like to make it right. We're issuing a goodwill credit to affected accounts, which will appear on your next statement automatically — no action needed on your end. If you have questions, or believe you were affected and don't see a credit within seven days, please reach out to our support team and we'll take care of it directly. Thank you for your patience and for continuing to trust us.

Subject: Yesterday's payments outage — what happened and what we're doing about it

Yesterday, our payments service was unavailable for approximately four hours. During that window, some of you saw transactions fail, get stuck mid-flow, or return errors at checkout. That is not the experience you trust us to deliver, and we're sorry. If your business or your customers were affected, the responsibility for that sits with us.

The root cause was a misconfigured cache invalidation rule that, under a traffic spike, caused our payment authorization layer to repeatedly evict and rebuild critical state. As load climbed, the service degraded until transactions began failing outright. Once we identified the faulty rule, we rolled it back, restored cache stability, and confirmed full recovery before standing down our incident response.

To prevent a repeat, we're making two concrete changes. First, configuration changes to the payments path will now go through canary rollouts, so a bad rule reaches a small slice of traffic — not all of it — before we catch it. Second, we're expanding chaos testing in staging to specifically exercise cache invalidation and traffic-spike scenarios, so this class of failure is caught before it ever reaches production.

If you were impacted, we'd like to make it right. We're issuing a goodwill credit to affected accounts, which will appear on your next statement automatically — no action needed on your end. If you have questions, or believe you were affected and don't see a credit within seven days, please reach out to our support team and we'll take care of it directly. Thank you for your patience and for continuing to trust us.
